title: Dome Valve "Low Seal Pressure" Alarm: What to Check When the Seal Isn't Broken
summary: Your system is locked out by a "Low Seal Pressure" alarm, but upon inspection, the inflatable seal is perfectly intact. Discover the true physical causes—from pressure switch drift to micro-leaks in the air lines—and learn how to quickly troubleshoot this deceptive fault.
content:
The On-Site Pain Point: A Deceptive Alarm

It is a highly confusing situation for any maintenance engineer: The control system throws a critical "Low Seal Pressure" alarm, halting the entire pneumatic conveying process and locking out the machinery. Assuming the worst, you disassemble the Clyde Dome Valve expecting to find a ruptured inflatable seal. But when you inspect it, the rubber seal is completely intact, with no signs of wear, tearing, or puncture.

Why is the system insisting the seal is losing pressure when the seal itself is perfectly fine?

The Underlying Physical Truth: It's the Peripheral Pneumatics

When you encounter this specific scenario, the fault rarely lies with the valve body or the seal itself. The true culprit is the peripheral pneumatic detection circuit.

For a dome valve to operate safely, the control system relies on a dedicated Pressure Switch located within the pneumatic control panel. This switch acts as a sensor, monitoring the air pressure being fed into the seal. Its job is to tell the PLC, "The seal is fully inflated and it is safe to proceed."

If the pressure switch does not detect the required setpoint (e.g., 4.0 bar) within a specific timeframe, it triggers the "Low Seal Pressure" alarm to prevent the system from operating with an unsealed valve. Therefore, if the seal isn't leaking, the pressure drop—or the failure to detect pressure—must be happening somewhere between the air supply and the seal's inflation nozzle.

Actionable Troubleshooting Recommendations

When the seal is intact but the alarm persists, focus your troubleshooting efforts on the pneumatic control loop. Follow these steps:

1. Calibrate or Replace the Pressure Switch

Over time, mechanical pressure switches can suffer from "setpoint drift" due to vibration or component fatigue. A switch originally set to trigger at 4.0 bar might drift and require 5.0 bar to activate, causing false alarms. Check the switch with a calibrated pressure gauge. If the setpoint has drifted or the switch is unresponsive, recalibrate it or replace it with a more reliable digital pressure switch.

2. Inspect the Supply Air Lines and Fittings

Trace the small-diameter air hose running from the pneumatic panel to the dome valve. Look for:

  • Micro-leaks or pinholes caused by abrasion against vibrating machinery.
  • Loose push-to-connect fittings that may be slowly bleeding air.
  • Kinks in the hose that restrict the volume of air reaching the seal.

Even a microscopic leak in this line will prevent the pressure switch from reaching its target setpoint.

3. Check the Inflation Nozzle O-Rings

The inflatable seal connects to the external air supply via an inflation nozzle (or insertable air nipple). This connection relies on tiny O-rings to maintain an airtight seal between the rigid valve housing and the rubber seal. If these O-rings are crushed, degraded by oil, or missing, air will escape directly at the connection point. The seal won't inflate fully, and the pressure switch will trigger the alarm. Inspect and replace these O-rings if necessary.

Conclusion

A "Low Seal Pressure" alarm does not automatically mean a ruined seal. By understanding how the control system monitors inflation pressure, you can avoid unnecessary valve teardowns. Always check the pressure switch, the air lines, and the nozzle O-rings first to save valuable maintenance time.
